title: Observation of sine-Gordon-like solitons in a spinor Bose-Einstein condensate
authors: Yannick Deller, Alexander Schmutz, Raphael Schäfer, Alexander Flamm, Florian Schmitt, Ido Siovitz, Thomas Gasenzer, Panayotis G. Kevrekidis, Helmut Strobel, Markus K. Oberthaler
abstract: We experimentally generate sine-Gordon-like solitons in a spin-1 spinor Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) utilizing a robust and reproducible local phase-imprinting scheme. We find that the soliton velocity can be tuned by the effective quadratic Zeeman shift. This enables the investigation of controlled soliton interactions, in which we observe the characteristic elastic collision behavior of the integrable sine-Gordon model. The spatial displacement -- the so-called phase shift -- between incoming and outgoing solitons, the signature of their pairwise interaction, is found to be in quantitative agreement with numerical spin-1 simulations within the error bars. These results establish spinor BECs as a highly controllable experimental platform for studying aspects of the dynamics of sine-Gordon-like models.
summary: This paper demonstrates the experimental creation and manipulation of sine-Gordon-like solitons in a spin-1 Bose-Einstein condensate. By tuning the quadratic Zeeman shift, the researchers controlled soliton velocity and observed the elastic collisions characteristic of integrable models. The results provide a robust platform for studying nonlinear wave dynamics and effective field theories in ultracold gases.
main_problem: The study aims to experimentally generate and investigate the dynamics of sine-Gordon-like solitons in a spin-1 Bose-Einstein condensate, specifically focusing on controlling soliton velocity and observing elastic collisions.
main_result: The researchers successfully generated sG-like solitons, demonstrated that their velocity is tunable via the quadratic Zeeman shift, and observed elastic kink-antikink collisions with a phase shift that matches numerical simulations.
method: Solitons are generated using a local phase-imprinting scheme with a steerable laser, while the quadratic Zeeman shift is controlled via microwave dressing. The dynamics are validated through 1D spin-1 Gross-Pitaevskii mean-field simulations.
model_or_system: A quasi-1D spin-1 87Rb Bose-Einstein condensate in a box trap, described by a spin-1 Hamiltonian including kinetic energy, quadratic Zeeman shift, and spin-dependent interactions, which maps to an effective sine-Gordon field theory.
key_observables: Spinor phase (phi_S), transverse spin length (|F_perp|), spin components (F_x, F_y), and soliton velocity (v).
important_parameters: Quadratic Zeeman shift (q), spin healing length (xi_s approx 7 um), spin speed of sound (c_s approx 70 um/s), and ferromagnetic interaction regime (c_1 < 0).
main_assumptions: The effective sine-Gordon theory assumes constant atom densities, an assumption that is violated during the actual collision process.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Visualization of the soliton as a phase-kink; Fig 2: Phase imprinting process and comparison of experimental vs analytical profiles; Fig 3: Linear dependence of soliton velocity on the quadratic Zeeman shift; Fig 4: Visualization of a kink-antikink collision; Fig 14: Plot of velocity vs quadratic Zeeman shift; Fig 15: Extraction of phase shift from simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the experimental setup and imprinting technique, demonstrates the generation and stability of solitons, explores the control of velocity via the Zeeman shift, analyzes collision dynamics and phase shifts, and concludes with a theoretical comparison between the experimental results and the sine-Gordon model.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly controllable experimental platform to study integrable models and nonlinear dynamics, specifically demonstrating how many-body systems like spinor BECs can realize effective field theories like the sine-Gordon model.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Visualization of the sine-Gordon soliton embedded in the spin-1 system. The soliton is a smooth phase-kink in the spinor phase φS (see main text for definition), depicted as the red curve. Advancing the spinor phase amounts to a rotation in the spin-nematic plane, which is spanned by the spin observables Fx and Qyz. With the phase-kink profile, this leads to a Bloch domain wall structure in the transversal spin. Here we consider the case where the spin is com- pletely polarized along the Fx-direction for ease of visualization.

FIG. 2. Single sine-Gordon soliton in spinor phase φS and transversal spin length |F⊥|. (a) The one-dimensional system is initially prepared homogeneously in Fx. Applying a local magnetic field via the vector Stark shift of a steerable laser beam to one half of the system ad- vances the spinor phase, thereby rotating the spin in the spin-nematic plane spanned by Fx and Qyz. The smooth kink in the spinor phase profile is created by the Gaussian edge of the imprint laser, see also Fig. 13 for a numerical study. (b) Experimental data of isolated non- moving solitons for up to two seconds evolution time after prepara- tion, showing the length of the transversal spin order parameter F⊥. The...

FIG. 3. Linear dependence of the soliton velocity v on the quadratic Zeeman shift q. In the inset (a), the experimental data for (post shift) q/h = 0.53 Hz are shown. In inset (b) a conceptual visualization of the moving soliton in the framework of the spin-continuity equation is shown, where a q-dependent source term co-moves with the soli- ton.

FIG. 5. Phase-shift extraction in the elastic regime. The obtained velocity for the incoming solitons is vin = (16.8 ± 0.3) µm/s. In or- der to extracted the phase shift we assume energy conservation which yields δx = (6.3 ± 0.6) µm. This is consistent within the error with the value extracted from spin-1 numerical 1d simulations, which is 6.2 µm.

FIG. 4. Kink-anti-kink collision measured simultaneously in differ- ent observables. In the upper panel, the collision is shown in the transverse spin length |F⊥|. In the lower one, a gradual 2π phase change is visible in the spinor phase during the collision, which is in accordance with predictions for the sine-Gordon model. The quadratic Zeeman shift is given by q/h ∼0.5 Hz for this experiment.
